candidate_problems.text: How can 'organismal complexity' and the proposed 'dimension/topology of the regulatory-developmental transformation space' be defined in a precise, non-arbitrary, empirically usable way? The conjecture depends on several candidate proxies for complexity and several structural invariants of R and D, but it leaves open which quantities are load-bearing, how they relate, and whether a single formal measure can unify cell-type diversity, hierarchical organization, modularity, attractor structure, and developmental recursion.

Which invariants of regulatory architecture and developmental dynamics are actually high-leverage causes of large changes in complexity, and how robust is that claim across taxa? The conjecture names modularity, feedback structure, controllability, attractor number/stability, compressibility, and degeneracy, but it is unresolved which of these are explanatory versus merely descriptive, how they interact, and whether perturbations to them predict complexity changes better than simpler genomic or transcriptomic measures.

What is the correct treatment of environmental coupling in the genotype-to-phenotype map: is complexity primarily intrinsic to the genome-parameterized developmental system, or distributed across organism-environment interaction? The conjecture includes environmental coupling in the scaling law, but this creates an unresolved tension about where complexity 'resides,' how much developmental organization is outsourced to ecological structure, and whether two genomes with similar internal architectures can realize very different complexities under different environmental scaffolds.
